CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The ‘“ Irish °’ Times was quite cross with the outcome
of England’s Faithful Senate’s hysterical movement.
““ We deplore,’” it declared, ‘“ from every point of view
the outcome of yesterday’s meeting of the Standing

'ommittee of the Senate of the Royal University.”’

We can see in our mind’s eye the gloater over the
proposed robbery of 22 Irish seats tearing its three
rs as it screeches with impotent passion:‘‘ The crass
upidity of this campaign against the outward symbols
alty t?, the King is, perhaps, its most exasperat-
ature. §

1, 4if! Keep cool; or if you c'a.n’t, keep
ou can. Tt will be time enough to get

beside yourself when the DarLy LEADER appears on the
scene.

Alf winds up its article in this way:—‘‘ The young
men who took part in that demonstration have had, it
would appear, a lucky escape from righteous punish-
ment. We hope they will be properly thankful for it,
and that a little reflection will cause them to feel
heartily ashamed of themsclves.”  4lf was mnot so
lucky in escaping from righteous punishment when it
libelled the parish priest of Killaloe. It had to pay
dearly for its anti-Catholic escapade; whether or not
it is  heartily ashamed ’’ of itself for its cruel libel we
have our doubts, for it is questionable if Alf has any
shame in him; but we are sure that Alf is too keen a
commercial person not to regret sincerely that it lost so
much money as a consequence of its cruel libel.

Reginald Brabazon, Earl of Meath and Honorary
Colonel of the Zifth Battalion Royal Dublin Fusiliers,
has been in the Diplomatic Service. Sherlock Holmes
might have discovered as much without looking up a
reference book, for the Earl had a cold at the time of
the last meeting of England’s Faithful Senate. Cer-
tainly events were enough to give the poor man a
chill anyway. The physical condition of a man who
had just ‘“ proved to the world,”” as the Earl himself
might say, that he was an unmitigated genius, disposed
him, we should say, to catch a cold in this trying
weather.

The Earl of Meath, when that honorary soldier and
experienced diplomat has recovered from the cold,
should take a trip over to Bangor in Wales. There are
some ‘‘ill-conditioned > students in that part of the
world. We gather from a recent issue of the Man-
chester Guardian that it appears that a band of stu-
dents from the University College of North Wales,
Bangor, ‘‘marched down the thronged and narrow
street arm in arm, filling the whole width of the street.
By and bye they met a countryman coming to the fair
on horseback. They blocked his passage and not con-
tent with this began ‘larking’ with him, pulling his
legs, and so on. The horseman after a while lashed
out at them with his riding whip, and—apparently
prepared by last year’s incident—a band of farm ser-
vants and others, armed with sticks, suddenly appeared
on the scene and furiously assailed the students. The
contest which followed was disastrous for the students,
who in a short time were flying, caps in hand, in all
directions. Three of them received somewhat severe
wounds and were carried into the shop of Mr. Luther
Joues, chemist, who bandaged their wounds and after-
wards disposed of them by baclk roads.”

When the distinguished diplomat and honorary
soldier has succeeded in inducing these students of the
University College of North Wales to prove to ‘ the
world ’’ they have renounced street rowdyism he might
turn his attention to the drinking evil at Oxford. When
he has administered the anti-treating pledge at Oxford
he should start out with a good heavy ferrule on his
wattle and make straight for Cambridge. Some ‘ill-
conditioned >’ rowdy undergraduates from the Univer-
sity in that place kicked up a shindy and Police-Con-
stable Leonard Savidge was struck on the head with a
brick, and Constable Ridgewell appeared in Court with
a bandaged head. A student of Clare College pleaded
guilty to using obscene language and striking a police-
man. This undergraduate was fined 40s. for obscene
language' and £5 for assaulting the police. A student

There are presentations and presentations. 
Presen-
tatio!ls to policemen and bank clerks, who are being 
pensioned off or are being shifted on promotion, are

An A,ustralian reader, writing from the Shamrock 
Club, Melbourne, under date October 7th, says : -
" Dear Sir-I am taking the liberty of writing to you, 
and asking for an expression of your opinion on, I hope, 
a, not too difficult or personal subject. 
A few years ago 
we had a visit from the now Prince of Wales, then the 
Duke of York. 
There was a procession given-in hi~ 
honour through the streets of Melbourne ; in it walked 
the members of the Irish National Foresters, lead by 
one of their head members on a white horse waving a 
Union Jack while their band played 'God Save the 
Queen,' passing their beloved Royal Highnesses. What 
is _you opi~ion of such conduct oh the part of so-called 
Irishmen 1 
We fear it would be somewhat of an impertinence on 
our part if we poked our nose into a matter of this kind. 
Australians know their own business, and they would 
resent our interference as much as we might resent 
theirs in any policy conce1,ning Ireland. 
Born Austra-
lians, and Irish-born people who are Australians by 
adoption, are Australi'ans, just as the people of IrM1 
birth or parentage in America are Americans. 
We do 
not believe in the existenc.e of a " greater Ireland '' be-
yond the seas. 
Had , the Irish carried their language 
with them, an Ireland beyond the seas might conceivably 
exist. 
The battle of the Irish nation must be fought 
out on the soil of Ireland; and these people who have 
left it for Australia, America, or elsewhere are practi-
caUy childven of the country of their adoption, what-
ever that country· may be, and have ceased to be Irish. 
They may come back to this _country, ~nd r~sume their 
part as Irishmen; and we smcerely W'lsh tliat a great 
many persons of Irish extraction or Irish birth now liv-
ing as Americans· or Australians would return to Ire-
land with their skill and capital, and help those on the 
soil to work out the pi:oblem of Irish nation-making. 
As for men of Irish birth or extraction in Australia and 
America, they, no doubt, know their own business; and 
if some of them don't, it is not for us to tell it to them. 
The attitude of Australians towards the British Royal 
Hou~e is purely a matter for Australians; as we believe 
in Home Rule for Ireland, we willingly concede the 
right of Australians-even those Australians who may 
call themselves " Irish National Foresters '' -to solve 
their own political problems in their own way. 
That 
is the only answer 1Jhat we can give to our Melbourne 
reader.

CORRESPONDENCE.

GENEALOGICAL AND HISTORICAL MAP OF IRELAND.

Referring to the letter from “ Student’ in last
week’s issue, “ Wanted—An Historical Map of Ire-
land,”” Messrs. Corrigan and French, London, write
us drawing attention to the Historical Map of Ireland
with which their firm is prominently associated. They
say:—

It is based on Dr. Philip M‘Dermott’s Map of 1846,
supplemented by the subsequent one of Dr. R. R.
Madden.

“ Both Map and names have been corrected, verified,
and expanded by John G. Lee, the well-known heraldic
artist and authority on Irish genealogies. The Annals
of the Four Masters, the Tribes and Customs of Hy-
Fiuchrach, O’Dugan and O’Heerin’s Topographical
Poems, O’Hart’s Pedigrees, etc., etc., have all been laid
under tribute in the compilation of this Map. It is due
to you, sir, to inform you, as evidence of the widespread
circulation of the popular Irish Press, that its advertise-
ment has resulted in orders for the Map from all parts of
the world, notably from Australia, New. Zealand, India,
Canada, several parts of Africa, and even Hong Kong.

“Your readers will be pleased to learn, also, that there
is a steady and increasing demand for the Map in Ire-
land, not only from teachers and managers of ordinary
schools, but from professors in various colleges in Dub-
lin, Cork, and Galway.”’

IRISH IN THE NATIONAL 8CHOOLS.

HE following appeared as an advertisement in the
daily papers on the 9th inst.:—

“ Norice.—Inasmuch as it has been intimated to the
Commissioners of National Education that the Lords of
his Majesty’s Treasury cannot undertake to provide
funds for the teaching of any extra subjects after the
school-year ending in June, 1906, the Commissioners
give notice that they will be unable to pay these fees
after that date.”’

Although “extra subjects’’ are spoken of, Irish is
the subject aimed at.

“The “Notice’’ announces the doom of the Irish
special classes which, in recent years, have been estab-
lished in connection with National Schools.

In 1904, Irish was taught in 1,983 National Schools
to 95,487 pupils. In 1,185 of these schools special
classes had been formed, so that by teaching Irish as an
“extra subject ’—“before or after ordinary school-
hours **—fees could be claimed; and to the teachers of
these special classes £12,069 4s. 1d. was awarded. After
June, 1906, no such fees will be paid ; the 1,185 special
classes for Irish will disappear, and the teaching of Irish
during ordinary school-hours will languish.

THE LEADER.

NoveMBER 18, 1905.

The enemies of Irish on the National Board have acted
skilfully. The “ordinary school-hours’” have, at pre-
sent, a great number of subjects which must be taught,
but, needless to say, Irish is not one of these. TFailure
to teach such obligatory subjects successfully, debars a
teachor from obtaining an increment to his salary, and
from promotion to a higher grade. But with a zeal
which™ deserves praise the teachers of 1,983 schools
managed to find some time during “ordinary school-
hours ’’ for Irish. This, however, would be of little use
were it not that most of these teachers also established,
or were about to establish, special classes—* oybside or-
dinary school-hours ”’—for Irish. The fees paid by the
Board for such classes helped to cover the expenses con-
nected with them, and left a sum—a very small sum—
for the teachers’ remuneration. After June, 1906, the
fees for Irish as an “extra subject ’” will cease.

Plainly, however, the Commissioncrs are troubled by
the fear that Irish may still continue to be taught
“ during ”’ ordinary school-hours and without any hope
of payment. Numerous as are the “obligatory sub-
jects ”’—the subjects which must be taught in order
to obtain promotion, and even in order to escape cen-
sure—zealous teachers, as wo have said, have been able
to find some little time for Irish during ordinary school-
hours. ~ The Commissioners now take steps to render
such procedure practically impossible in future. They
do this by adding another to the already long list of
obligatory subjects. In 1900 the Commissioners
crushed out Mathematics from the School Programme ;
now they announce that “ A Course of Mathematics

will be required in all boys’ schools with
two or more teachers;’’ and in similar girls’ schools
“A Course of Instruction in Cookery and Domestic
Economy is strongly recommended.”” During ordinary
school-hours every National School must teach Reading,
Writing, Spelling, Arithmetic, Grammar, Composition,
Geography, Physical Drill, Manual Instruction, Object
Lessons and Elementary Science, Drawing, singing, and
in girls’ schools, Needlework ; in “ two-teachers’ ’’ boys’
schools Mathematics must be taught, and in “two-
teachers ’’ girls’ schools Cookery and Domestic Economy
are “strongly recommended.” Special classes “before
or after school-hours’’ are abolished. The Commis-
sioners may now look calmly on their scheme and say
to teachers, managers, and the public: “This is our
scheme, show us if your can, how Irish can now be*
taught; we have not expressly prohibited the teaching
of TIrish, but we defy you to find time for it!’’

It is useless to expect anything from the anti-Irish
majority which at present rules the National Board.
Whether the Treasury directs the Board, or the anti-
Irish Commissioners make suggestions to the Treasury,
and then pretend to act by the Treasury’s directions
makes little difference. An Irish Education Board
which is bitterly antagonistic to the Irish language, and
slavishly subservient to the English Treasury is below
contempt. Still the situation is not without hope. It
seems probable that before the fateful 1st July, 1906,
the present Government will have ceased to exist, and
its fall will be followed by a reformation of the “ Na-
tional Board.”

In the meantime managers and teachers should in-
crease their efforts on behalf of the Irish language.
The same advertisement which announces the with-
drawal of fees for Irish, intimates that junior Assis-
tants will in future be recognised in schools having an
attendance between 35 and 50.  There are several
hundreds of such schools, and managers when making
appointments should select young persons who have a
knowledge of Irish. S.

THE TEMPERANCE CRUSADE—ANSAPPEAL.
“DIEU LO VOLT!”—GOD WILLS IT.

“T" HESE words were the battle-cry of the old Cru-

saders when they went forth in their thousands
to free Jerusalem and drive back the Turks. The Holy
Land had been conquered and laid waste by the In-
fidels who persecuted the Christians, and spared not the
sacred Tomb of Christ.  The holiest of the “ Holy
Places *’ were desecrated by the “abomination of deso-
lation.”” At the call of Pope Urban II. and Peter the
Hermit, Catholic Europe rose up with a cry of indigna-
tion. Thousands and tens of thousands went forth to
avenge the outrage upon Christ and the Christian
Faith, and their battle-cry was—“ Dieu lo volt!”’ (God
wills it!) 3

Surely to-day there is work for a New Crusade—not
against the East and Infidel Turk, but at home here in
Ireland against the Drink Demon that enslaves this
once “Holy Land’’ of saints and scholars. And our
bishops and priests, and the good men and women of
TIreland, are now determined to organise this New Cru-
sade for the freedom of our nation from this tyrant.
True Irishmen at home and abroad—all the world over
—will join with them, and raise the loud, determined
shout of the Crusaders— God wills it! God wills it!"’

In this shout for freedom I desire to join my humble
voice, and I venture to make an appeal through the
columns of your great paper.

.For long years the drink traffic and drunkenness
haye been growing in our country, and, like an invading
army, have conquered town after town and Pprovince
after province until we see to-day the whole land
wasted and plundered and desolate; our cities cursed
with the ourse of sin, decay, and dirt; our country-

sides lifeless and lonely, roofless houses and deserted
lands where once were cheerful homes and a happy,
prosperous people.  “ With desolation is the whole
land made desolate.’”” And this is not the work of
Turk nor Briton, nor of bad landlords, nor crow-bar
brigade. No! The desolation of Ireland is the deso-
lation of drink and drunkenness. Irish drunkards and
tipplers and pledge-breakers and those drunkard-makers,
the treaters’’—they are the men and women who
have brought Ireland down. And it is against these
Ireland must now rise, and hurl herself with fiery de-
termination and war to the death! English invaders,
indeed, and Penal Laws and bad landlords have
wrought grievous injustice in our land, and dope us
shameful wrongs. But that Ireland should be blighted
and robbed and disgraced by those who call themselves
her own! The Penal Laws disgraced not Ireland, but
the persecutors themselves. They robbed us of lands
and homes and life, but the robbery only opened
heaven’s gate to Ireland’s martyrs. Drink and drun-
kenness, our drunkards and “ treaters,”’ disgrace our
own selves shamefully before the world, and give even
the unbelievers and the sneerers, occasion to blaspheme.
Drink robs Ireland of millions of money and millions of
children, flinging their bodies into graves and pits and
slums, into emigrant ships and workhouses and jails,
and sends their souls, not to heaven, but to damnation.
The Penal Laws made martyrs; the drunkards and
treaters damn. Ireland clasping kisses the green
graves of the victims of Penal Laws and evictions,
pitch-cap and packed jury—but she loathes the grave of
her drunkards and tipplers and treaters. Z'heir grave
smells to heaven. Rottenness shall be their covering.

Now, in the face of all this sin and shame and deso-
lation, will Irishmen who love God and their country
be silent and inactive? Have we not eyes to see and
cars to hear? The nation is dying. In God’s blessed
name, then, is it not the hour for the New Crusade
when we should arise like one man, and gird ourselves
to battle for the nation’s life against drunkenness and
every fatal custom that is perpetuating this cursed
vice? It is not to be a battle of words. It is not to be
a war of abuse against publicans. It must be a war
of valiant deeds against drink and drunkenness and
treating.  Those publicans who live on Ireland’s
poverty and traffic in the nation’s shame, they will go
down in the fight. It is better that hundreds should
suffer than let the nation die. And cannot a beggarly
remnant of a nation well afford to close 10,000 of its
24,000 public-houses? A poor people might well
afford to reduce by at least £7,000,000 its Drink Bill,
which reaches the huge total of £14,000,000 a year!
Irishmen pay these fourteen millions of money over the
public-house counters and in clubs, and about six mil-
lions of that money goes to the British Government as
the tax of a self-imposed slavery. Irishmen pay this
slavery tax, and when some hesitate to do so other
Trishmen are found “ to drag’’ them into public-houses,
and make them pay the tax. This slavery tax must be
paid no longer by a self-respecting people. Let Irish-
men pledge themselves against drink altogether, or
against treating, and that will end our slavery and
disgrace.

Here, then, is the work for Irish hands to do. Here
is the holy war for Ireland’s valiant Crusaders. You
have longed and waited impatiently for the hour when
Ireland’s war trumpet should summon you to battle.
The hour has now come, and your country calls you.
You who are ready to give your heart’s blood for Ire-
land, you are asked, not to give up your life for her,
but to give up drink, or at least to give up dragging
others into public-houses to drink. Ireland asks not
to-day for your blood, but for your pledge and the
example of a sober life. You are eager to go forth to
redden your sword in the blood of the foeman. Hold!
Slay rather the custom of treating in public-houses that
has done more than England’s dragoons to slay the

- Irish race. Listen to a few of Pope Urban’s burning

words to the bishops and priests, princes and soldiers

: *ff Europe assembled in their thousands at Clermont in

and apply them to yourselves:— Warriors, who

NovemsER 18, 1905,

hear me, you are incessantly on the look-out foi vain
pretexts for war. Rejoice! For behold before you now
a war that is lawful and just. The hour has now ar-

rived to test whether yours is a genuine courage. Let

those arms unjustly employed in massacring one an-
other be turned now to repel the enemy of the Chris-
tian name.  God, Whose eye will be upon you when
you are fighting a battle which is for His glory, will not
forget you. Soldiers of the living God, let no das-
tardly or profane hankerings of the flesh keep you
back. Be deaf to everything but the moans of Sion.”’

Wo are told that at the conclusion of Urban’s
solemn appeal the assembled multitude cried out with
a voice of thunder—“Dieu lo volt!” (God wills it!)
Thousands there and then took the Crusader’s Cross
and, pinning it upon their heart, swore to fight in th(::
Holy War for the freedom of Jerusalem.

Ireland’s Holy War is against Drink, the accursed
enemy of our nation—the “ Holy Land ”’ of saints and
scholars. The valleys and hills and the “ Holy Places”
of Ireland, made sacred by the preaching of the Gospel
and the presence of the-Mass, by the footsteps of Pat-
rick, Brigid, and Columcille, are now profaned and
made abominable by drunkards. Now has the hour
come to test whether yours is a genuine Ppatriotism or
not. If your patriotism is genuine, then put on the
Crusader’s Cross—the badge of Temperance, tee-
totalism, or the Anti-Treating League. Trample upon
every human consideration that stands between you
and your country’s redemption. Soldiers of Faith and
Fatherland, let no dastardly hankering of flesh and
blood keep you back. Let no sacrifice deter you. Be
deaf to every cry but the moans of your native land.
Rise up to-day at the call of Ireland’s leaders, and
fight in the holy war against the enemy of our Faith
and Fatherland. Let your motto be:—* For the glory
of God and the life and honour of Erin!” And your
battle-cry : —“ God wills it! God wills it!”’

THE PROPOSED IRISH ACADEMY.

OME time since Mr. Henry Morris invited criticism
on his shadowy scheme for Irish examinations
and an Irish Academy. Some writers in the LEADER,
notably “Pertinax’’ and “B.A., B.L.,”” offered the
required criticism; but from Mr. Morris’s reply it is
plain that criticism is the last thing on earth he would
be thankful for. Mr. Morris professes a preference
for constructive criticism.  Such  criticism should be
forthcoming in great abundance.  For constructive
criticism to the mind of Mr. Morris is building educa-
tional and academic houses not with earthly brick and
mortar, but with clouds and mist and fog.” There are
seventeen letters in the Irish alphabet—I suppose M.
Morris would reject h as being too material—every com-
bination of a pair of these letters—nay, every permuta-
tion of them—gives you a new degree in Irish learning;
these degrees could be grouped according as a vowel
came first, or a mute, or a liquid. . There are five
vowels corresponding to the cag ciigroe. The letters
called liquids—t, m, n, p—would correspond to the
four dialects, and so on. Then the whole could be
grouped together to form one vast academic course—
success in which would secure the happy graduate lite-
rary and philological infallibility and consequent
immunity from criticism.

The pgssibilities of such a scheme are endless; they
are as limitless as the unrestrained imagination of a
fireamor, and the scheme can be maintained, of course,
in ethereal disregard of ways and means. How mean
d.o the initials B.A., B.L., appear beside the blaze of
titles that can be got by means of the new Academy.
‘Why should it be not insisted on that every member of
the Academy should present a certificate that he has

got lessons for the space of two vacations in Patagonia,

and be required to present a treatise on the Malayans,

on Confucius, and, of course, on “ Julius Cesar, boil the

brandy?’ For the life of me I see no other way of
saving the Irish language. The old-fashioned plan of

He did so for several reasons-some of

the Deed, and as having added to them, yet Erasmus 
Smith, no doubt, advised by his lawyers, did not think 
that that Section had sufficiently secured the money to 
Christ's Hospital'; for the Charter tells us that he had 
drawn up a tripartite Indenture between his Trustees, 
himself, a,nd the Governors of Christ's Hospital, fucing 
this grant, the lands out of which it was to issue, and 
the Charitable uses to which it was to be applied. 
This 
Indenture proves-although such proof is not neces-
sary-that the lands given in ·the Deed were confirmed 
to the Trustees by the Act of Settlement, and that the 
85th Section of the Act of EJrplanation did not, in. any 
way, invalidate their title. 
It would seem to prove 
also that the 85th Section of the Act of kplanation did 
not alter the other Trusts of the Deed. 
The legislature 
could as easily transfer property as alter Trusts. If it 
did not transfei· property by language purporting to 
say that it did transfer it, how did it alter Trusts by 
language pUl·porting to say that it did alter them 1 
But this does not affect tµe main question, as we shaU 
see later on. 
Another clear reason for the Bill of 1664 is that 
Erasmus Smith sought thereby to secure to himself 
power to -transfer to Christ's Hospital, London, more of 
the Endowments thl!-n £100 per annum. 
For in the 
three Certificates of the Court of Claims, .in the Lette1·s 
Patent of 1667, and in the Charter, he reserved to him-
self a right of applying to other pious uses, to be deter-
mined by himself, a moiety of the su,rplus that should

2 THE LEADER.

Temain after the Trusts declared had been fulfilled.
And from the letter of his Trustees to him, dated the
7th of August, 1681, and his reply to them in the same
year, we know that he sought to transfer, by Deed,
another slice of the Endowment to Christ’s Hospital.
Evidently with a view to this purpose, he urged the
Trustees to raise the rents, and to give no lease, except
in Limerick, and for building, longer than three years,
until the rents should reach £600. For this purpose
. also—namely, to increase the surplus to be carried away
by him, he reduced the number of Free Schools from 5
to 3. And although he mado this reduction on the
pleas of clothing poor children in the schools, and of
apprenticing them, we know from the Trustees’ letter
of 1681, referred to above, that before a.penny was ex-
pended on clothing or apprenticing the poor children
in the schools, he sought to take away a slice of the
Endowment to Christ’s Hospital, London.

A third clear reason is, that by getting more lands
for the Endowment than he had given in the Deed, he
wished to increase the moiety which he was determined
to carry away to Christ’s Hospital, London. Y

A fourth clear reason is, that by putting into the Bill
of 1664, more lands than he had given to his Trustees,
he secured that they should not surrender one-third of
what they had in obedience to the 5th Section of the
Act of Explanation.

A fifth clear reason is, that, by giving in his Bill of
1669, or stating that he gave 11,052 acres, plantation
measure, equal to 17,902 statute acres, to the Endow-
ment, he put forward a strong claim for more lands.
How many acres would he not require to enable him to
give to charity 17,902 acres?

Now his motive for the Bill of 1664, which we can
reasonably infer, is, that by the Bill he sought to obtain,
and did obtain, 4,776a. 2r. 4p. in the County of Louth,
over and above what he was entitled to, for his defi-
ciency as an Adventurer.

In sustainment of this motive, we have the following
established facts:—

1. Erasmus Smith obtained in the County of Louth
4,776a. 2r. 4p. more than he was entitled to, in order
to satisfy his deficiency. For the Deed tells us that
his deficiency was land for £2,700. Land in Louth was
sold at 12s. an acre, so that he was entitled to 4,500
acres. But the 5th Section of the Act of Explanation
enacted that Adventurers were to retain, or get, only
two-thirds of the lands they possessed, or were entitled
to, on the 7th of May, 1659. Erasmus Smith was thus
entitled to only 3,000 acres in Louth, whilst he received
there 7,776A. 2r. 4p. or 4,7776A. 2R. 4p. more.

2. By the Deed, Erasmus Smith gave to his Trustees
only 2,874 acres—all of which was in his possession at
the time he gave it.

3. Erasmus Smith put into the Bill of 1664 8,178
acres more than these 2,874 acres. For as we have
seen, he put into it, as having been given by him to the
schools, 11,052 acres.

4. These 11,052 acres were in the possession of the
Trustees, or of those under whom they did claim, on
the 7th of May, 1659. This is stated and admitted by
the Commissioners in the three Certificates of the
Court of Claims. Now, on the 7th of May, 1659, the
Trustees were in possession of only the 2,874 acres
given by the Deed of 1657 in Galway, Roscommon, and
the Isles of Arran. Therefore, the remaining 8,178
acres given, or alleged to have been given, by Erasmus
Smith in the. Bill of 1664 for the Endowment, were
then, on the 7th of May, 1659, in the actual possession
and occupation of persons other than the Trustees. The
lands in the Isles of Arran were not sold to the Earl of
Arran until some time between 1662 and 1665. The
sale is sanctioned and confirmed in the Act of Explana-
tion, passed in 16656. Who were the others who pos-
sessed the additional 8,178 acres given, or alleged to have
been given, in the Bill of 1664? We know from the
second Clrtificate, dated the 14th of J- uly, 1668, that of
the 8,178 acres given in the Bill of 1664, 30674. 3r. 14p.
were in _the possession of Adventurers other than Eras-
mus Smith on the 7th of May, 1659. For that Certi-
ficate tells us that all those lands were reprisals. And
we know that reprisals were the third part of lands

NovEMBER 18, 1905,

possessed by Adventurers on the 7th of May, 1659.
The words of the Certificate are:—“1It appeared also
to this Court that the lands, tenements, etc., hereafter
mentioned were lands, etc., seized, sequestrated %
on account of the late horrid rebellion "’—this is good:
from the regicide rebels—“and thereby forfeited to,
and vested in, his Majesty CharlesI., and were by this
Court adjudged unto the common stock of reprisals.
And it further appeared to this Court, that by the
aforesaid Bill ’’ (that of 1664) “the lands, tenements,
etc., hercafter mentioned were intended to be set forth
for the several charitable uses therein expressed.”’

The 2nd Certificate tells us that four Adventurers
whom it names, had surrendered as reprisals 1453, 3g.
95p. in the Counties of Limerick and Sligo, of the
3,0674 3r 14p given in the said Certificate. From whom
were the remaining 1,614 acres taken as reprisals? Not
from the Trustees, because they were still deficient of
their two-third part; and the 85th section of the Act of
Explanation had specially forbidden reprisals to be
taken from them. Not from Erasmus Smith, because
he had already assigned the lands for the Endowment
in the Bill of 1,664, and the 85th Section of the Act of
Explanation had specially forbidden any lands assigned
or ‘“mentioned ”’ in the Bill for the Endowment, to be
taken away. Therefore, those 3,067 acres given by the
2nd Certificate were in the possession of Adventurers
other than Erasmus Smith, not only on the 7th of May,
1659, but in 1664. And thus Erasmus Smith magna-
nimously gave to the Endowment, in his Bill of 1664,
3,067 acres which he knew at the time belonged to
other Adventurers, and were possessed them. Becausz
they were mot taken as reprisals from those Adven-
turers until 1665, by the Act of Explanation.

The 1st Certificate, dated the 21st of September, 1666,
and the Letters Patent of 1667, tell us that the seven
Trustees were, in 1666, in possession of the 3,264 acres
given to them by that Certificate, and that some of those
acres, were, on the 7th of May, 1659, in the possession
of Adventurers other than Erasmus Smith, or his Trus-
tees. Now, omifiting the question, how many of these
3,264 acres were in the hands of persons other tham
Erasmus Smith, and his Trustees, on the 7th of May,
1659, and adding to these 3,264 acres, the 3,067 acres
given by the 2nd Certificate, we find that 4,721 acres of
the 11,052 acres given in the Bill of 1664, remain stilf
to be accounted for.

Did those 4,721 acres belong to him when he gave
them to the schools? Everything that we know about
him tells us that they did not. We have seen that
when he gave 2,874 acres to the Endowment by the
Deed,he secured to himself more than £800 a year out
of them, whilst he gave to the Endowment out of them
only £300 a year. Would he have given to the Trustees
4,721 acres more of his own lands and secured to him-
self nothing? We have seen from the 2nd Certificate,
that he gave in his Bill of 1664, to the Endowment,
3,067 acres, which he knew at the time, belonged te
other Adventurers, and were in their possession. Tl{e
reprisals were not taken from those Adventurers un§1l
the following year by the Act of Explanation passed in
1665. We are, then, perfectly justified in concluding
that Erasmus Smith pub those 4,721 additional acres
into his Bill of 1664, knowing at the time that they be-
longed to, and were possessed by other Adventurers.
This conclusion is strengthened, if not confirmed by the
fact, that we find no trace of any claim put forward by
Erasmus Smith to recover those 4,721 acres. Would he
have given up all those lands without a struggle, if
they had belonged to him  'We have seen how he got
a special section, section 86, into the Act of Explana-
tion, in order to recover from Sir John Stephens 1429
acres of inferior mountain land in Dromhane, Tip-
perary. Would he have quietly submitted to be de-
prived of 4,721 acres of profitable. lands? He would
not. D.H.

NOVEMBER 18. 1905,
THE LONDON A0NAC.

G HE London Gaelic League has good reason to be
pll‘oud of the unqualified success of the third
© dona¢ it has organised. The great Exhibition and
Sale which was held in Bishopsgate Institute on Novem-
ber 4th and 6th was, in every way, a marked advance
. on pre'vious years, the exhibits being much more repre-
sentative of Trish industries, the attendance of buyers
being all that could be desired, and the arrangements
completely satisfactory.
: It is a genuine pleasure to attend the Aonac for
) -~ there one meets all that is best of Ireland in London,
B and though it is painful to contemplate the fact that
such a splendid body of carnest, enthusiastic, practical
workers are exiles from the old land, still their magnifi-
cent example to Irish men and women at home is such
a power for good that their loss to Ireland is greatly
diminished ; and the only regret is, that our people at
home are not more conversant with the daily efforts and
sacrifices young Irish men and women in London are
making to forward the interests of Ireland by their un-
ceasing work in the Industrial and Language Crusade,
What a revelation it would be to some of our hopelessly

Anglicised towns if a contingent of the Seomini and

the half-hearted Gaels were to visit the inspiring scene

of the dona¢, and there find, in the very heart of Lon-

don, three thousand Irish men and women coming to-

gether with the greatest enthusiasm at the bugle call

of the Gaelic League, many of them giving weeks and

months to the organisation of the huge undertaking, and

all eagerly purchasing the various articles offered for

sale, thercby helping on, as they most heartily desire to

do, the efforts of Irish manufacturers to provide employ-

ment for Treland’s sons and daughters in Ireland. The

Aona¢ was organised on new and extensive lines this

year. In addition to Irish manufacturers it was de-

cided to admit London shopkeepers who stock Irish

materials. Several London merchants availed of the

opportunity to bring themselves under the notice of

Irish Ireland, and to solicit their custom. In doing so

they acted wisely, as in all cases there was a very brisk

= trade, and, as a single instance of the volume of business

done, it may be mentioned that one firm, who sold

Irish poplin ties (and other similar articles), took in £37

in the two days. In the tailoring trade there were

several London exhibitors of Irish goods, foremost

amongst them being the pioneer firm of Messrs. Corri-

gan and French, whose consistent advocacy of the pro-

ducts of Irish mills for several years past has fairly en-

titled them to the splendid support and patronage

which was accorded them at the dona¢.  This firm

made a complete purchase of the greatly admired ex-

hibit from the Dripsey Woollen Mill, and the enter-

prise was a very wise one as the Dripsey cloths were

rapidly purchased at the sale, the famous “ Kathleen

Ni Houlihan ”’ costume cloths and the Dripsey caps
being in great demand.

- The Irish convents were well represented in the lace,

rug and hosiery sections, and poor Sir Horace

will be pleased to hear that the nuns from Loughglynn

~ (where there is an “Irish Ireland Factory in a Land-

lord’s Mansion ”’) were completely sold out of the fine

exhibit of rugs which they brought over. Irish print-

ing firms were well represented by Duffy and Co., and

Browne and Nolan. Pierce’s world-renowned bicycles

were to the fore, while among the other prominent ex-

 hibitors were the firm of Fitagerald’s, Cheapside; Bar-

rington’s, Goodbody’s, Dun Emer industries, Ballyvour-

ney knitting, Glenbeigh carpets, Malone Bros.,

‘Mara and Sons, Shaw and Sons, Varmn’s,. Fqxford

en Mills, etc., etc. The youngest of Irish indus-

as reprosented by the firm of MacMonamin Bros.,

.P.”” polishes are rapidly polishing the im-

ed articles off the Irish market.

THE LEADER.

While the dona¢ provided purchasers with almost
every commodity they required, it will strike the reader
that a great number of the Dark Brothers are not in-
cluded in the above exhibitors. The caves still have
charms for some Irish manufacturers, and the chance of
capturing the enormous market of London could nof
induce them to come into the light. None of the
“ great ’’ woollen mills were there, though some of them
are hungering for orders, and putting off hands weekly !
No ready-made clothes factory condescends to let us
know of its existence. ’

During the ona¢ an informal Conference was held
at which Mr. Boland, M.P., presided, and at which the
manufacturers or representatives at the Aona¢ and pro-
minent London Gaels attended. An interesting discus-
sion took place as to the best “ next step’’ to take in
pushing on the Industrial Movement in London.
Various views were expressed, and the general opinion
favoured the establishment in London, and in various
districts where there is a large Irish population, of In-
dustrial Associations on the lines of the parent associa-
tions in Cork. It was decided to recommend the Lon-
don Gaelic League to take steps to initiate the
movement,.

It is sincerely to be hoped that the Cork Conference
next week will result in appointment of an industrial or-
ganiser. An organiser is a ‘ long-felt want,”” and the
time is now ripe for a man. - The work there is for him
to do would fill a page. The happily named “ Chamber
of Horrors,”” which is to show us at Cork Exhibition
some of the frauds that are being faked in Ireland at
present suggests one sphere of activity, but it is in the
eminently practical work outlined above for London
workers that the great need of an organiser becomes ap-
parent, for no permanent results can be hoped for if
voluntary workers are entirely depended on.

The London Aona¢ has in three years done an incal-
culable amount of good work for the Irish firms that
have availed of it. When will Glasgow hold its
< onsa¢—and Manchester and Liverpool? Here is where
the oragniser comes in. If the Cork Association could
spare its splendid Secretary, Mr. Riordan, for a few
months organising in London, the results would be as-
tonishing. Many of the small, struggling industries
cannot afford to send a representative to the donac,
and consequently they do not exhibit at all. This is.
regrettable, and the difficulty should be bridged over
somehow. Is there any reason why the Department
should not be requested to contribute towards the ex-
penses of the dona¢ , and thereby do something practi-
cal? Sentimental objections will be urged against such
a step, perhaps, but the Department’s money is ousr
cwn money, and we have a right to a voice in its ex-
penditure. A very small sum would help to safeguard.
the Gaelic League from the risks it runs in financing
the annual donac, and much more beneficial results.
could be achieved for the smaller industries if the
League had a guarantee against loss. There are some
good Irishmen in the hidden recesses of the Department.
who, if they were free of the red-tape that binds them.
would be of far greater service to the Industrial Move-
ment working with the Gaelic League, and the Indus-
trial Association than they will ever be in the dark cor-
ridors of Merrion Square; and if the Cork Conference
can induce Sir Horace to bring his Department down
from the clouds, and let free some of his experienced
Irish officials (men like W. M‘Cartney Filgate, for
example, who were revivalists before there were any
dona¢, Associations, Leagues or Departments), to work:
in harmony with the wonderfully successful Committees
of Cork and Dublin, there is no doubt but that the co-
operation of the unprogressive, but wealthy, Depart-
mont with the popular and practical Associations and.
Leagues would be of considerable and immediate benefit
to the Industrial Movement.

Liam.

able air to serve as a National Anthem.

sideration and discussion. 
I therefore think it worth

character.

God

Putting these two aside once for all, we ask-which one 
of our national airs is best suited to . be our National 
Anthem ? 
The better to answer this question let us first deter-
mine what are the qualities to be looked for in such an 
air. 
I venture to specify the following. It shoul€l be 
(1) serious; (2) stately; (3) not aggressively war-like;

The first two qualities need 1,1ot,

As to the third, a National

pression of the national home life and national aspira-
tions, an embodiment of the past, present, and futm·e of 
the race.

unsuitableness of an ancient modal air for part-singing 
and band arrangement, to which, of course,· a National 
Anthem should lend itself. 
Finally the advan.tage of a 
chorus in a National Anthem is obvious. 
And now which of our airs possesses the above quali-
ties in the most marked degree? Fully a quarter of a

The air I

unfortunately has some unpleasant memories clinging

words the last traces of them would disappear. 
I mean 
" The Boys of Wexford." 
The unhappy split of some 
yea-rs ago did at least · one good thing-it taught the 
whole country this magnificent air. It seems to me to 
. have all the i,equisite qualities in a high degre~-
It 
is serious. It is stately-no one who has heard it su~g 
by great crowds could fail to reali~e the breadth and 
grandeur of its various strai_ns. 
While n?t exactly war-
like it is bold and assertive, as a National Anthem 
should be. It is thoroughly Irish in tone, and yet suffi-
ciently modern 
for ord~nary har~on_i_c . ti·eatment. 
Finally it, not merely admits of a chorns, it absolutely 
demands one...'....a chorus, too, that will be " like the sound 
of many waters.'' 
. . 
, 
'.Dhis is my selection, and I now submit ~t to the 
opi-nion of readers of the LEADJ!lR, 
The questi_on of the 
words will also have to be discussed; but it may be 
allowed to ~tand over for the present. 
The natural 
order would seem to be t0 decide upon the tune first, 
and then have the words written for it, perhaps by com-
petition among the . whole "sea.-divided Gael." They

LITERATURE AND SCORN ONCE MORE. 
I 
AM very . glad that I wrote that article on " The 
Irish Scorn of English Literature," if for no other 
re:,ison than that it brought forth froin "Imaal" one of 
the best of the many vigorous and intellectual articles 
which he has contributed to the LEADER. 
I ·might point to "Imaal's" contribution, "Irish 
Ireland, Literature and Scorn," and say, almost with-
out qualification, as the Yankee said:-" Them's my 
sentiments." 
• .But before I say anything on this point, I must fulfil 
my promise to reply to the article by" C." in your issue 
.of 28th uito. 
To begin, " C. " shows himself by his letter to be a 
typical exampie of the scorners of English literature to 
whom I alluded in my first article. 
I presume from his 
st,atcments that he has actually read the works of Maz-
zini, and Rousseau, and Emerson, and Carlyle. That 
being so, I am driven to a.sk the question-" Did he 
understand them 1" because he heaps scorn upon the 
heads of men who do not need my poor recommenda-
tion to show that they are admitted by the greatest 
intellects of all nations to be world-powers in the do-
main of letters. 
The man who denies the intellectual and literary 
powers of Mazzini or Rousseau must, in my opinion, be 
absolutely devoid of literary taste. 
But what are we to 
think -0f his strictures on Emerson and, Carlyle? 
. 
Has be read Emerson's Essays (including t_hat won-
derful composition, " The Over-Soul ") and h_1s poem~ 7 
H he :has, I can only pity his lack of apprec1at1011; . 1t 
would be .futile to argue with him. 
"Emerson's wnt-
ings," says "C.," "are· Pantheism in doctrine, and 
confusion in style." 
His writings, let me say, are not all Pantheism; but 
why should we fear Pantheism in Emerson while we 
embrace it in Gaelic? Gaelic writings are full of Pan-
theism and Pythagorism; and Pantheism and Pytha-
gorism are as harmless in Emerson as they are in 
Gaelic. 
With regard to Emerson's· style, it is lucidity itself-
to anyone who can understand him; but all men. can-
not understand him, any more than all men can under-
stand a.nd appreciate the music of Wagner or the · pic-
tures of Turner. 
• 
" C." does not believe in Carlyle either. 
I am sorry 
for him. Has he read "Sartor Resartus," " French Re-
volution," or "Past and Present "-to mention a few 
of Carlyle's master-pieces 1 Is there not style in these? 
I contend there is,· and individual style of the first order. 
In fact, Carlyle has one of those luminous, distinc-
tive, vigorous, individual styles which are the., envy of 
imitative literary aspirants, and after the acquisition of 
which hundreds of writers have toiled, and shall yet 
toil, in vain. 
• 
Carlyle's is a marvellously powerful style; I can use 
no weaker adjective. 
I do not agree with " C." that the choice of models is 
a small thing ; it, is a very great and a very important 
thing. 
Neither am I in entire accord with "C's." remarks 
about " n. centre of higher learning in this country in 
harmony with the bent, the genius, the aspirations and 
ideals of our people." 
I think there is too much 
whining on the University Question. 
Some of the 
greatest duffers I have known have been University 
men; and nearly all the geniuses the world has pro-
duced have been either men who never saw the inside of 
a University, or who were geniuses in spite of their 
having gone through one of those institutions whose 
chief function, it seems to me, is to turn men out all to 
one pattern, with all their individuality crushed out of 
them. He is a lucky man who escapes unmaimed. 
And now to return to " Imaal;" as I said, I am 
almost in entire agreement with his article. 
" Imaal " 
says : -" As a people we do not scorn English literature, 
nor any other literature ; we simply do not. read litera-
ture at all." 
This remark-coupled with " C's." statements--cou-
firms my arguments ; because the existence of the scorn 
of literature is apparent; and that the scorn applies to 
English literature is also apparent from the fact that 
we have had practically uo other literature to scorn. 
" How can we buy books while an alien Government 
forces- us to spend fourteen million pounds a year on 
spirituous refreshments,'' exclaims " Imaal.'' 
Now, that is scarcely fair to the alien Government . . 
It is putting the saddle on the wrong horse.

. MR. LOUIS VICTORY AND ENGLISH LITERATURE. 
I 
SHOUL-D be very sorry to say anything hurtful to 
the feelings of Mr. Louis Victory. 
I am convinced 
he is actuated by the best of motives in adjlll"ing his 
fellow-countrymen 
to turn to the study of the best 
models in European literature. 
On the other hand, 
I cannot admit that he is a good judge of what are the 
master-pieces of human thought and expression. 
He 
applies the epithets, " narrow and uncatholic," to such 
men 
as 
Newman, 
Montalembert, 
Bossuet, 
and 
Fenelon. 
By doing so I should imagine he rather con-
victs himself of being narrow and uncatholic himself. 
Take away Newman and all who are inferior to him in 
Bnglish literature, in the power of appealing to uni-
versal human interest, and where 'is E.nglish literature 7 
There is hardly anything left. 
I confess that once upon 
a time in my raw youth I was taken by Carlyle, and 
more or less believed in the oracle. 
Since then I have 
found salvation. 
Now Carlyle is to me the apostle of 
brute force, and in himself an intellectual monstrosity 
and a moral_ monster. He is utterly inferior to New-
man. 
So is Ruskin. 
There is· much in Ruskin I 
ad-
mire; but as a teacher of manl:iud, as a leader of intel-

20 THE LEADER.

lect, there can be no comparison between the two men.
Surely anyone who has read the Essay on Development
or the Grammar of Assent, to say nothing of that won-
derful human document, the ‘Apologia,” must ac-
knowledgg Newman to be among the kings of the
human mind. I lay no stress on his being the creator
of the Church of England, as it is at the present time.
He was that before he reached his ripest powers.

Then again, Montalembert is contemptuously dis-
missed by Mr. Victory. Montalembert was an histo-
rian, and what English historian will Mr, Victory put
in his niche? All my life I have been a lover of his-
tory, of the history of many nations, and I am sorry to
say I think very poorly of English historians in com-
parison with those of other countries. They are party
men almost to a man. The spivit of party is upon
them, or rather in them, as a possession. I include
Lingard in my description, although ho was more free
from the influence of the imp than most of them.
Montalembert’s work is not perhaps of the highest
order; but what Englishman could write such a work
as he wrote it? e sought the truth wherever he could
find it, and he showed an impartiality which even
Venerable Bede could not show. What Englishman
would give such a picture of the works of the Irish
monks such as the chivalrous Frenchman has given?
We Irishmen owe Montalembert an immense debt of
gratitude, and I wish we would try to pay it in the
way he would like best by reading his “ Monks of the
West.”” I feel a bit indignant with Mr. Victory for
slighting such a man. How different he is from Gb-
bon! In the first place, like the veritable Saxon pirate
that he was, Gibbon picked the brains of Tillemont in
all directions, and took to himself the borrowed glory
of profound research. Then he was the party man from
the scalp to the sole.  Certainly, Englishmen do not
shine in historical literature.

Mr. Louis Victory has his own canons by which to
discern what is literature and what is not. I wonder
how far he will himself be guided by those canons.
One of them is that a mere sectarian appeal will not
make its way to the universal heart of humanity. Such
a canon as this will still further narrow the field of
literature within very modest dimensions. Whatever
may be said for retaining the Synoptic Gospels, St.
John’s Gospel certainly falls within the forbidden cate-
gory. It was certainly polemical. So were the epistles
of St. Paul for the most part, and those of St. James
and St. Peter. The Judaising sect among the early
Christians called these into existence.

The writings of the Fathers of the Church must also
be dropped from the domain of high literature. They
were, almost all of them, apologetic and polemical.
Does Dante owe nothing to them? Yes, indeed, Dante
does. And so do Shakespeare and Milton and Goethe
and Carlyle and Emerson, however much they may all

canons is that the one-creed man has no claim at all for
admission into the temple of literary fame. Must then
a writer be a man of no creeds or of all creeds? I have
never yet been able to discover the man of no creed.
Voltaire had a creed, and so had Diderot, and Julian
the Apostate. Libanius, the preceptor of Julian, had
a very defined creed which excluded the poor from ail
participation in the benefits of life. He would have ex-
cluded the denizens of Grub Street and the votaries of
Bohemia and the Latin Quarter. I know one man who
believes in all creeds even when they contradict one an-
other flatly. He calls himself an English Goth, and
swears by Odin and Thor. He believes in the gods of’
Olympus and the fetiches of Dahomey. He swallowed
with a gulp Crom Cruagh and his sub-gods twelve when
I introduced him for the first time to the Plain of
Adorations in Cavan, but he made a wry face about it,.
it was so hard to accept anything Irish.

But what about the one-God man? Can he become
a master-mind? It is surely as narrow and uncatholic
to believe in one God as to hold one creed. Did not
Moses have an axe to grind when in the still regions of
the desert he proclaimed— Hear, O Israel, the Lord
thy God is one Lord?’ Mahomet’s creed re-echoed it
long centuries after. Drop out all Christians and
Mahometans, the schoolmen of Paris and the Arabian
philosophers, and it strikes me we shall have to go to
China for our literature if we are to be bound by the
Canons of Mr. Louis Victory.

Once upon a time I thought a lot of English litera-
ture. I was young then, and unacquainted with any
other. T have been obliged to rescind my judgment
since. That there is an abundance of talent and clever-
ness about it goes without saying, but that there is any-
thing like solid principles in it, anything that grips the
human conscience, or bows the human mind before its
sovereignty, or lifts the human heart from the gross
things of earth to the good that is abiding and the
beauty that is unfading, and the union that is not dis-
solved, it is almost imperceptible. English literature
is really a sloppy literature. It is wanting in sub-
stance and reality. Take Emerson for example. The
American writes very brilliantly. He describes the
surface of things with the brush of an impressionist
artist, but in the name of goodness what is it all
about? All is vague, undefined, barren, and with alb
